instruction: Rewrite the content to meet insurance regulatory and compliance standards.

input.draft_text:
Who Can Change the Beneficiary on the Life Insurance Policy?
Keywords: beneficiary, insurance beneficiary, beneficiary for life insurance
Meta Description: To secure the future of your loved ones, picking the right beneficiary for your life insurance plan is essential. Additionally, major life changes may necessitate changes to this list. Read on to find out more about how to pick the right beneficiary and how to amend your beneficiary list.
A life insurance policy allows you to secure the fiscal well-being of your family members in the event of your untimely demise. If your family is chiefly reliant on your income, the death benefits of such policies take care of its financial requirements after your passing. However, to avoid payout confusion, you must enlist beneficiaries for your life insurance policy as soon as you purchase it.
Understanding a Life Insurance Beneficiary
When you buy a life insurance plan, you are asked to enlist an insurance beneficiary. This individual will essentially be eligible to receive the insurance payout from your policy, once you pass. In other words, the death benefit that you stand to derive from the policy will be paid to your listed insurance beneficiary. Thus, it is important to put a lot of thought into picking the right beneficiaries for your insurance policy.
Selecting Apt Beneficiaries for Life Insurance Plans
It is important to note that you are not bound to name just a single individual as your insurance beneficiary. To ensure that your death benefits reach your loved ones, you can designate multiple individuals as contingent beneficiaries to your policy or even enlist trusts to handle your payout benefits. If you enlist more than one beneficiary, you will also have to decide what percentage of the payout they’ll be entitled to.
While picking a beneficiary is a personal matter and requires a lot of thought, it should ideally be someone who is directly impacted by your passing away and would be capable of handling the payout efficiently.
For instance, if you live with a significant other, you would want their financial future to be well-protected after your passing. Thus, most policyholders pick their significant others as their primary insurance beneficiaries. Additionally, if you’re planning on naming your children as primary beneficiaries, they have to be over the age of 18. If they are underage, you would have to appoint a guardian for them.
Who Can Change the Beneficiary on the Life Insurance Policy?
When talking about insurance beneficiaries, it’s equally important to note that you can change beneficiary names with the changes happening in your life. For instance, if you’ve recently married, you would want to make your spouse the primary beneficiary for your existing life insurance plan.
However, there are a few caveats to consider. Since life insurance policies are legally-binding contracts signed between the policyholder and the insurance provider, only the policyholder can make such changes.
Reassessing your beneficiary list after every major life change is a good choice as this ensures that the people who are closest to you remain protected at all times. While you can make such changes as many times as needed, you must ensure that the names of your beneficiaries are correctly spelled to avoid future confusion.
Beneficiary Payout Options
While picking the right beneficiary is important, determining the right payout route is equally significant. Your death benefits can be paid out to your beneficiaries in the following 3 ways:
Lump-Sum Payment- This is the most common type of payout option where the insurer pays your death benefits as a lump-sum amount to your beneficiary. This helps beneficiaries use proportions of the payment for settling outstanding debts, paying mortgages, college fees, etc.
Instalments and Annuities- If you’ve listed your spouse or children as your insurance beneficiary, this payout option can help act as a regular source of monthly income for them. Moreover, a large lump-sum amount may be difficult to manage for some beneficiaries. In such cases, a staggered payment option is better suited. Here, a part of the sum assured may be paid as a lump-sum amount, while the rest of it is paid to your beneficiary in monthly instalments over the next 10-15 years.
Retained Asset Account- If you pick this payout option, you essentially leave the death benefit amount with the insurance company in an interest-bearing account. Thus, this amount will qualify for certain interest and your beneficiaries will be provided with a chequebook to access the amount as and when needed. This may be a prudent option for those looking to grow their death benefit payouts while also retaining access to the sum.
Wrapping it Up
From handling your mortgage payments to your kids’ college expenses, the lump-sum death benefits from your life insurance policy can help manage the expenses of your family members after your demise. Since this remains the chief rationale behind investing in such policies, picking the right beneficiary and payout option can be the keys to the future financial security of your family. Both these decisions need to be based on your specific life goals and circumstances.
The first step in this process remains policy selection. Thus, whether you opt for a whole life or term plan, selecting policies with simplified claim settlement processes is important to help your loved ones receive the sum assured from the policy in a hassle-free way.
